unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Bring together your strongest vocabulary, reading, grammar, speaking and writing from Units 1-9. Create a polished English product for a real audience, present it clearly, respond to questions and reflect on your Grade 4 growth.
unit.learningPath: Choose evidence. Review language. Plan and draft. Improve from feedback. Present. Reflect. Complete the final course quiz.

visual.alt: A Grade 4 learner presenting a completed English portfolio

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Letters and packages that are sent or delivered.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I received a letter in the mail today.
practiceSentences: I received a letter in the mail today.
The postman brings mail to our street every afternoon.
Has the mail come with news from Aunt Halima?
"Put the mail on the kitchen table," said Mother.
I once got birthday mail with a card from Kenya.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - i - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'mail', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A way people talk and write to share ideas and feelings.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: English is a language that many people speak around the world.
practiceSentences: English is a language that many people speak around the world.
Every language has its own special sounds and words.
How do babies learn the language of their family?
"Somali is the language we speak at home," said Adam.
I taught my friend a greeting in my family's language.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - a - n - g - u - a - g - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'language', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who belongs to a country and has rights there.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Every citizen has rights and rules to follow.
practiceSentences: Every citizen has rights and rules to follow.
Each citizen of our town helped clean the park.
How does a citizen show respect for the law?
"A kind citizen welcomes new neighbours," said the teacher.
My father became a citizen after living here ten years.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - i - t - i - z - e - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'citizen', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To have the same opinion, or to say yes to something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I agree with you that reading is fun.
practiceSentences: I agree with you that reading is fun.
The whole class had to agree on one team name.
Can we agree to take turns on the swing?
"I agree that honesty matters most," said Samira.
My cousins and I agree that mangoes beat apples.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - g - r - e - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'agree', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Daily Life & Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The air you take in and let out of your lungs.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I took a deep breath before I started to speak.
practiceSentences: I took a deep breath before I started to speak.
Theo held his breath as the results were read.
Can you see your breath on a frosty morning?
"Take a slow breath before you begin," said the teacher.
I caught my breath after racing up the steep hill.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - r - e - a - t - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'breath', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The clear water that comes from your skin when you are hot.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: After running in the sun, I wiped the sweat from my face.
practiceSentences: After running in the sun, I wiped the sweat from my face.
Drops of sweat rolled down Sami's face during practice.
Why does sweat help cool your body down?
"All that sweat shows you worked hard," said the coach.
My shirt was damp with sweat after the long run.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - w - e - a - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'sweat', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small amount of water in the air or on a surface.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The grass was wet with moisture after the rain.
practiceSentences: The grass was wet with moisture after the rain.
The desert plants store moisture inside their thick leaves.
Where does the morning moisture on the grass come from?
"Check the soil for moisture before watering," said Grandfather.
I felt moisture in the air just before the storm.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - o - i - s - t - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'moisture', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The top or outside part of something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The surface of the lake was calm and shiny.
practiceSentences: The surface of the lake was calm and shiny.
Raindrops made rings on the surface of the pond.
Why is the surface of ice so slippery?
"Wipe the surface of the table, please," said Mother.
I polished the wooden surface until it shone brightly.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - u - r - f - a - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'surface', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature & Weather.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Food made of two pieces of bread with meat, cheese, or vegetables inside.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I ate a chicken sandwich for lunch.
practiceSentences: I ate a chicken sandwich for lunch.
Samira packed a cheese sandwich for the school trip.
Would you like a tuna sandwich or an egg one?
"This chicken sandwich tastes wonderful," said Theo happily.
I cut my sandwich into four small triangles.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - a - n - d - w - i - c - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'sandwich', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A young sheep, or the meat from it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We cooked lamb and rice for the guests.
practiceSentences: We cooked lamb and rice for the guests.
Grandmother cooked tender lamb stew for the family dinner.
Did you see the little lamb following its mother?
"The lamb needs fresh water," said the farmer.
I helped feed the lamb during our farm visit.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - a - m - b
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'lamb', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Small white or brown grains that people cook and eat.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She ate rice with her chicken.
practiceSentences: She ate rice with her chicken.
The pot of rice steamed gently on the stove.
Do you prefer brown rice or white rice with fish?
"Add the rice when the water boils," said Mother.
I spilled some rice while filling the big bowl.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - i - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'rice', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A part of a plant, like pepper, that adds flavour to food.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We added some spice to make the soup tastier.
practiceSentences: We added some spice to make the soup tastier.
Each spice in the market had its own strong smell.
Which spice makes the soup taste so warm?
"A little spice wakes up the flavour," said Aunt Maryam.
I sneezed when the spice powder tickled my nose.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - p - i - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'spice', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Food and Health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Help or work done for others.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The library started a free book service for the town.
practiceSentences: The library started a free book service for the town.
The bus service stops near the library every hour.
What service does the fire station give our town?
"Helping elders is a noble service," said the teacher.
I joined the library service that lends books to villages.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - e - r - v - i - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'service', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Community and Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To bring something to a person or place.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The mail carrier will deliver our letters.
practiceSentences: The mail carrier will deliver our letters.
The baker will deliver fresh bread to the shop.
Can you deliver this note to the head teacher?
"We deliver groceries on Fridays," said the shopkeeper.
I helped deliver dates to our neighbours during the holidays.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - l - i - v - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'deliver', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Community and Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Facts or details about something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The website has useful information about our town.
practiceSentences: The website has useful information about our town.
The poster gives information about the school book fair.
Where can we find information about desert animals?
"Check the library for more information," said Mrs Ali.
I collected information about whales for my project.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - n - f - o - r - m - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'information', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Community and Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: An idea or opinion in your mind.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She shared her thought about the new plan.
practiceSentences: She shared her thought about the new plan.
Adam shared a kind thought during circle time.
What thought crossed your mind when you won?
"That is a clever thought," said the teacher, nodding.
I wrote down every thought for my story plan.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - h - o - u - g - h - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'thought', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Community and Communication.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To run fast on four legs, like a horse.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The horse began to gallop across the field.
practiceSentences: The horse began to gallop across the field.
The young horses gallop across the open field at dawn.
How fast can a zebra gallop when danger comes?
"Watch the ponies gallop past the fence," said Theo.
I heard the horses gallop past our gate last night.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - a - l - l - o - p
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'gallop', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Action and Movement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To go faster, or to make something go faster.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The car can accelerate when the driver presses the pedal.
practiceSentences: The car can accelerate when the driver presses the pedal.
The train began to accelerate as it left the station.
Why do cars accelerate slowly up a steep hill?
"Feel the plane accelerate before take-off," said Father.
I watched the motorbike accelerate down the empty road.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - c - c - e - l - e - r - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'accelerate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Action and Movement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To go forward or to keep going.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: After the bell rings, we will proceed to the playground.
practiceSentences: After the bell rings, we will proceed to the playground.
The visitors may proceed through the gate after signing in.
Shall we proceed with the quiz after break?
"Please proceed to the hall quietly," said the head teacher.
I waited for the green light before I proceeded.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - o - c - e - e - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'proceed', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Action and Movement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A shape that curls round and round, moving out from the centre.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The snail moved in a spiral on the ground.
practiceSentences: The snail moved in a spiral on the ground.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - p - i - r - a - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'spiral', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Action and Movement.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who cleans and takes care of a building.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The janitor keeps our school clean and tidy.
practiceSentences: The janitor keeps our school clean and tidy.

"Thank the janitor for polishing the floors," said Mrs Ali.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: j - a - n - i - t - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'janitor', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who builds things from wood.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The carpenter made a beautiful wooden table.
practiceSentences: The carpenter made a beautiful wooden table.

"A careful carpenter never wastes wood," said Grandfather.
I watched the carpenter smooth the door with sandpaper.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - a - r - p - e - n - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'carpenter', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who does hard work, often with their hands.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The laborer helped build the school walls.
practiceSentences: The laborer helped build the school walls.
Each laborer carried bricks across the busy building site.

"Every laborer deserves fair pay," said my uncle.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - a - b - o - r - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'laborer', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who designs or builds machines, roads, or buildings.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The engineer drew plans for a new bridge.
practiceSentences: The engineer drew plans for a new bridge.
The engineer tested the bridge before cars could cross.
What does an engineer use to draw exact plans?

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - n - g - i - n - e - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'engineer', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Feeling worried or a little afraid about something that might happen.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I felt nervous before my big test at school.
practiceSentences: I felt nervous before my big test at school.
Maya felt nervous before reading her poem to the class.
Why do you feel nervous before a big match?
"Being nervous means you care," said Mother kindly.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - e - r - v - o - u - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'nervous', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Feeling very worried, with lots of worried thoughts.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She was anxious about being in the dark.
practiceSentences: She was anxious about being in the dark.
Theo grew anxious when the bus was very late.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - n - x - i - o - u - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'anxious', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Very, very scared.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I was terrified when I saw the huge spider.
practiceSentences: I was terrified when I saw the huge spider.
The mouse looked terrified when the cat crept closer.
Were you terrified during the loud thunderstorm last night?
"I was terrified of the dark once," admitted my brother.
I felt terrified on the tall slide, but I tried it.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - e - r - r - i - f - i - e - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'terrified', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Not sure that something is true or will happen.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: He felt doubtful about winning the game.
practiceSentences: He felt doubtful about winning the game.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - o - u - b - t - f - u - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'doubtful', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Emotions, Behaviour, and Identity.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Things that are used for a special job or task, like tools or machines.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We need special equipment to play football, like a ball and goalposts.
practiceSentences: We need special equipment to play football, like a ball and goalposts.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - q - u - i - p - m - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'equipment', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A cover or container used to hold papers and keep them tidy.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I put my homework in my folder so it does not get lost.
practiceSentences: I put my homework in my folder so it does not get lost.
Samira keeps her best stories in a purple folder.

"Label your folder before you lose it," said Mrs Ali.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - o - l - d - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'folder', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Tools, Machines, and Everyday Items.

readings.title: Review text 1: Fair Effort, Daily Gains
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Every morning, Sophia helped her mother sweep the floor and boil the water before school. She liked to stay busy, because she believed that small tasks showed big effort. She also tried to work at speed, so that she still had time to read.
At school, the head teacher pinned up a notice. “Writing Contest!” it said. “Write a report about someone in your community. The winner gets a dictionary!”
Sophia smiled. She wanted to improve her language and master new words, and a dictionary would help her learn faster. But she still had homework, chores, and her daily reading. “I must find a balance,” she told herself. “If I use my time well, I can do it all.”
That evening, her mother asked, “Can you manage this contest and your schoolwork?”
“I think so,” said Sophia. “I will try my best and continue learning.”
She thought about who to write about. There were many important people in her village, but one stood out — Mr. Mzee, the kind citizen who worked at the posthouse. He sorted and delivered the mail every day.
After class the next day, Sophia went to visit him. “Mr. Mzee,” she asked, “may I interview you for my school report?”
“Of course,” he smiled. “That sounds like a good project.”
“What do you do in your daily work?” she asked.
“I sort mail, help people send letters, and talk to visitors. I also read letters for people who cannot read well.”
“Why is mail still important?” asked Sophia.
“Because not everyone has a phone. A letter can bring peace, and it can make someone feel remembered.”
Sophia asked if he spoke more than one language. “Yes,” he said. “I had to master English and Kiswahili. Some people speak local languages too. It is necessary, so that I can help everyone.”
“And do you do this job every day?” Sophia asked.
“Yes,” he said. “It is my usual work. Some days are busy and some are quiet, but every day matters.”
Back home, Sophia began her report. She wanted it to be fair and honest, so she added all the details he had told her. She checked her spelling and used her neatest handwriting.
Her father saw her writing. “Take your time,” he said gently. “Do not leave out anything important.”
“I won't,” she replied. “This report has a purpose.”
The next morning it rained hard. A student shouted, “The contest is cancelled!” But the teacher said, “Don't worry! We will hold it in the library. Bring your reports!”
The students sat in a circle and took turns reading. Some were nervous. Others forgot their lines. Sophia waited quietly. When it was her turn, she stood up and read in a clear voice:
“Mr. Mzee is a helpful citizen who delivers the mail and helps others read. He speaks two languages and works with care. He told me that daily effort helps us gain trust and knowledge.”
After she finished, one judge asked, “What did you learn from this?”
“I learned to listen, to write clearly, and to keep going even when it rains,” Sophia said with a smile.
Later that day, the teacher made the announcement. “The winner is Sophia! Her report was honest, clear, and full of meaning.” The whole class clapped, and Sophia stood proudly, holding her prize — a brand-new dictionary.
At home, her mother hugged her. “You did it!”
“Yes,” said Sophia. “I did not stop. I worked hard, and I found a good balance.” She opened her new dictionary and pointed to a word. “Look — ‘Effort: work done with care.’ Now I know the meaning in my heart.”

readings.title: Review text 2: The Storm and the Science Tent
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Every year, Noah looked forward to the village science fair. His school set up a big tent near the lake, where students shared their projects with the whole community. This year, Noah and his friend Sophia had planned a weather display. They wanted to teach people how storms work and how to stay safe.
On the morning of the fair, the sky already looked strange. A grey, foggy mist covered the path. “I can barely see the ground,” Sophia said. “We should walk slowly.”
Noah nodded. “It's the foggiest morning we've had all year.”
When they reached the lake, the tent was ready. Their teacher, Mr Noah, was arranging the students' posters. “Set up your display near the solar light,” he said. “The sun isn't strong today, but it will still help.”
Nadia unrolled their poster about hurricanes, volcanoes and tornadoes. “This one shows a tornado moving across a field,” she said. “It spins faster than any other storm!”
Noah added, “And this picture shows a volcano with hot lava. It is dangerous, but it is also beautiful.”
People began to arrive. Some students talked about meadows, others about the lake or the forest. A younger boy pointed at Noah's display. “What is a canyon?” he asked.
“It's a deep valley,” Noah said. “Much deeper than a meadow. A river can run through it. A canyon can be the deepest land on Earth.”
Mr Noah clapped his hands. “Time for a safety quiz! Sophia, your turn.”
Nadia stood up. “What causes a hurricane?” she asked.
A girl raised her hand. “Warm water and strong wind?”
“Correct!” Sophia smiled.
“Now Noah,” said the teacher.
Noah pointed to a picture. “Where does hail fall from?”
“From the clouds during a storm,” a student answered.
“Right,” said Noah. “And hail can damage crops, so we must always watch the weather.”
Just then, the wind picked up. The surface of the lake began to ripple. A few parents looked up at the sky.
Suddenly, a man shouted, “It's coming back! The storm from last week!”
Some children began to panic. “Don't run!” Mr Noah said firmly. “Let's all move inside the hall. Quickly, but calmly.”
As they packed their things, Noah felt something cold on his cheek. “Hail!” he said. Small balls of ice bounced on the ground. “Let's go!”
They reached the hall just as the wind began to howl outside. Mr Noah closed the door. “We're safe here,” he said.
Nadia sat down, catching her breath. “My heart is beating fast,” she said. Her shirt was wet with sweat.
“That was scary,” said Noah. “But it was also kind of exciting.”
Inside, they carried on with their weather talk. They showed how animals use thick fur to stay warm in snowy weather, and how moisture in the soil helps plants grow.
“Rust forms when metal gets wet and is left too long,” Sophia explained. “Like that old nail outside.”
“And mold grows on food when we leave it too long,” Noah added. “That's why we always cover our lunch boxes!”
One girl pointed at a chart. “What's that tall tree near the bay?” she asked.
“That's a royal palm tree,” Sophia said. “It grows near the water. It is tall and strong, like a king's tree.”
Noah smiled. “Nature is powerful,” he said, “but we can learn to understand it.”
When the storm finally passed, the sky cleared. Sunshine filled the room. “Look,” a student said. “The light is shining brightly now.”
Mr Noah smiled. “You all did well today. You learned, you taught, and you stayed calm. You showed real courage.”
Noah and Sophia packed up their project. “Even with the storm, this was the most exciting science fair ever,” Noah said.
“And we didn't even have to roam far to learn something new,” Sophia laughed.
Outside, puddles sparkled in the sun. The wind had slowed — but the lesson had stayed.

readings.title: Review text 3: The Bitter Lunch
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
In the hot fields of Sudan, Sami helped his uncle grow rice and care for the cattle. Every morning before school, he fed the cows and carried water from the well. It was hard labor, but his uncle always smiled and said, “We work hard now to eat well later.”
One day at school, Sami's teacher stood at the front of the class. “I have a project for you,” she said. “Each of you will write a story about food, and it must carry a health message. You must use our new words and write with care. You might even share your story at the community lunch.”
Sami raised his hand. “Can it be a true story?” he asked.
“Yes,” the teacher said, “as long as it teaches something helpful.”
That evening, Sami walked to the village bakery. It smelled of warm bread. He was hungry, so he bought a sandwich filled with salty meat, chewy cheese, and plenty of spice.
“I should tell you,” said the baker, “this meat is not fresh. But it still tastes good.” Sami was too hungry to care.
At home, he shared part of the sandwich with his cousin Sarah. She frowned. “I think this meat is from sick cattle,” she said. “There is a strange smell.”
“You worry too much,” said Sami, and he ate the rest.
Late that night, Sami's stomach hurt badly. He felt heat in his belly and cold in his hands, and he could not sleep. Sarah heard him groaning and woke her father.
At the health center, a kind nurse checked him carefully. “This may be food poisoning,” she said. “Some chemicals are used to clean old meat, and they can be dangerous if they are not washed off.”
Sami groaned. “I only ate a sandwich. It had a lot of spice, but the meat may have been old.”
“You must be more careful,” said the nurse gently. “Old meat and dirty tools can lead to disease. Eaten too often, bad food can even lead to a serious sickness like cancer. Always check your food first.”
The next day, Sami stayed home from school. His uncle brought him warm soup and said, “You are lucky this was not deadly. Always check what you eat.”
By the afternoon, Sami felt better. Then he remembered his story. He sat outside with his notebook and wrote:
“I once ate a bad sandwich. It looked fine, but it was not fresh. It gave me a sore stomach, and I learned to check what I eat.”
He thought for a while, then wrote more: “Some chemicals help to clean food, but too much is harmful. Farmers must use less pesticide, and people must wash their food with care.”
“What else should I write?” he asked Sarah.
“Gather some facts about food and health,” she said. “Then give good advice.”
Sami smiled. “That helps.” He added a final part: “We eat to feed our brain and our body, and good food helps us grow. Too much alcohol or dirty food can make us sick. A famine shows us how precious food really is, and how we must protect it.”
On the day of the community lunch, Sami read his story aloud. A few children laughed at first — until he reached the part about his illness. Then the room grew quiet, and everyone listened.
“Thank you for that, Sami,” said the teacher. “Now, who else would like to share?”
Hannah raised her hand. “Sami also learned where to put a comma in a list of food!” The whole room giggled.
Then the village elder stood up. “Food is a gift we must not waste,” he said. “Let us not waste it, and let us never eat without checking it first.”
As the food was served — lamb, rice, and sweet dates — Sami chose only what looked clean and safe. The meal was simple, but everything was warm and fresh.
Sami leaned over to Sarah. “This is the freshest lamb I have had in weeks,” he whispered.
She grinned. “That is because I helped to cook it.”
Sami took a bite and smiled. This time, his stomach did not hurt at all.

readings.title: Review text 4: The Circular Plan
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Amal lived in a small town near the coast of Ghana. Her school was plain on the outside, but it was full of energy inside. Every week, the teacher gave the class a new challenge — something to learn or build together as a team.
One morning, the teacher wrote on the board: “Project: Create a way to share information with the community.”
Amal raised her hand. “Like a newspaper?”
“Something like that,” the teacher said. “But you must use what you already know. Use your knowledge, and make something useful.”
The students talked during break. “We could build a big sign!” said Adam.
“No,” said Selma. “People will just walk past it. Let's make a box with papers inside.”
“That's still too plain,” Amal added. “It has to be circular. That's my rule!”
“Why circular?” Adam teased. “Is your brain a wheel?” Amal laughed, but she did not answer. She already had an idea.
After school, Amal went to the local library. She looked at the location of every fiction shelf, every news board, and every reading chair. She found a dusty corner near the door and smiled. “There is space here,” she whispered.
The librarian came over. “Can I help you?”
“We want to deliver news and stories to the whole town,” Amal replied.
“That's a big job,” the librarian said kindly. “But good service always starts small.”
The next day, Amal and her friends arrived early. They brought cardboard, tape, scissors, and markers.
“What are you building?” asked a younger boy.
“A circular news station!” Amal answered. “People will stop here and spin the circle. Each part gives different information — the weather, stories, or health tips.”
Adam frowned. “That sounds hard.”
“It's a challenge,” Amal agreed. “But we can do it.”
The teacher came by and nodded. “Very creative,” she said. “Now check your spelling before you stick anything down. And don't forget to erase the pencil lines.”
The group worked neatly. Amal drew the circle. Adam wrote the headlines. Selma added a funny comic about a scientist and a dancing goat, and everyone laughed.
“We need facts too,” Amal said, “not just fiction.”
“There is a doctor in town,” said Adam. “Let's write about health.”
“There are new shops opening as well,” added Selma.
“Write clearly. That is our top priority,” Amal reminded them.
By Friday, the project was ready. They placed it near the school gate. It stood tall and colourful.
The next morning, a group of parents and children gathered around it. “What is this?” asked a man.
“It's called ‘The Circular Plan',” Amal explained. “Turn it, and read what's on each side.”
A woman read aloud, “Tips for clean water.” A boy spun it and found the comic. “A goat that becomes a scientist?” he laughed.
“It's just fiction!” Adam shouted, grinning. People laughed and clapped. “This is good service,” someone said. “Our children are teaching us.”
One parent pointed. “But there is no news about the market. That's what people care about.”
“Then please help us write it!” Amal replied. “We want real thought from the people too.”
Another woman smiled. “That's a good priority — sharing everyone's voice.”
The teacher stood beside the project. “This,” she said, “is how we build a community. We learn, we teach, and we grow. Today, our students became leaders.”
As the crowd left, a boy began to tease Adam about his handwriting. “Your words look like little bugs!” he laughed. Adam grinned. “I wrote them in a hurry. I'll fix them later.”
“Good idea,” said Amal. “We must always check for quality.”
That afternoon, Amal looked at the circle again. “It may look plain,” she said to Selma, “but the work inside it matters.”
Samira nodded. “It's not about how big the quantity is,” she said. “It's about what people learn.”
And from that day on, people in Amal's town stopped by the spinning circle, learned something new, and sometimes even added their own page.

readings.title: Review text 5: The Spiral Cave
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
One afternoon, in a quiet village near the forest, three children — Ibrahim, Isaac, and Sami — were playing beside the river. They often made up games and told stories, but today they found something strange. Behind a pile of rocks, there was a dark hole in the ground.
“Let me peek inside!” said Isaac.
Sami held his hand. “Be careful. It might not be safe.”
But Ibrahim had already taken a step forward. “It is dark, but I can see something. The floor goes down in a spiral.”
“Like a staircase?” asked Isaac.
“Yes! Let us explore it!” said Ibrahim, feeling excited.
The children looked around for a strong stick and a torch. “We must check that it is safe,” said Sami. “We do not want to fall.”
They went in slowly. “First, peek inside. Then, proceed carefully,” whispered Ibrahim, copying what their teacher always said in science class.
Inside, the air was cold. The spiral tunnel turned and turned. Isaac pointed to the wall. “Look! A drawing of people… and a lion?”
They kept walking until they reached a wider space. “It is a cave!” shouted Sami. Their voices echoed off the stone.
Suddenly, they heard a soft sound.
“Was that a whistle?” asked Ibrahim.
“No,” said Isaac. “That was an animal!”
Then, out of the shadows, two eyes appeared. A small dog — thin and shivering — stepped forward. It wagged its tail.
“It looks hungry,” said Sami. “We have to rescue it!”
Ibrahim reached into his bag and pulled out a piece of bread. He called gently to the dog. “Come here, boy.”
The dog sniffed and walked closer. “I think he is friendly,” said Isaac. “He does not want to hurt us.”
“We should give him a name,” said Ibrahim.
“Not yet,” said Sami. “First we need to check that he is not sick.”
The children carried the dog out of the cave. Outside, the sun was low in the sky.
“We must accelerate,” said Isaac. “It is getting late!”
Back in the village, they went to the animal helper, Mama Hannah. She was kind and wise.
“Where did you find him?” she asked.
“In a spiral tunnel near the river,” said Ibrahim.
“You were lucky,” said Mama Hannah. “That tunnel is very old. There is pressure from the soil above it. It could fall in at any time.”
“We did not know,” said Sami. “We were too excited.”
“Next time you must tell your parents first,” she said. “But I am glad you brought him to me.”
The dog was given clean water and food. He wagged his tail and let Sami squeeze his paw gently.
“He is strong,” said Mama Hannah. “He only needs rest.”
“But who left him there?” asked Isaac.
“We may never know,” said Mama Hannah. “Sometimes people are greedy. They want an animal but do not care for it. That is not right. An animal like this can suffer when no one looks after it.”
The children were quiet. They did not like the thought of anyone being so unkind.
“We can help him,” said Sami. “Let us make posters and describe him. Someone might be looking for him.”
“That is a kind plan,” said Mama Hannah.
That night, Ibrahim wrote in his notebook:
“We found a cave, a spiral path, and a small dog. We learned to move slowly and to think. We felt pressure and fear, but we stayed strong.”
The next day, the children shared their story at school. Their teacher smiled. “You followed instructions, stayed calm, and made good choices. That is something to be proud of.”
“What should we name him now?” asked Isaac.
“How about the rescued dog?” said Sami. “He is strong, like a lion.”
“Perfect,” said Ibrahim. “He is our little rescued lion.”
the rescued dog barked — happy, and safe at last.

grammar.title: Sentence accuracy audit
grammar.explanation: Review agreement, tense and complete sentences.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Every sentence needs a clear subject and verb.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Correct six sentences from your portfolio.

grammar.title: Tense control
grammar.explanation: Check that past, present and future time are clear.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Yesterday I visited. Today I learn. Tomorrow I will present.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Revise six verbs and explain each tense choice.

grammar.title: Questions and responses
grammar.explanation: Use accurate Wh- and yes/no questions.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Where did you learn this? Why is it important?
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Write and answer six audience questions.

grammar.title: Description and precision
grammar.explanation: Improve nouns, verbs, adjectives and adverbs.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: The thoughtful learner explained the idea clearly.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Joining ideas
grammar.explanation: Connect ideas with and, but, because, so and when.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: I revised my story because feedback helped me.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Final editing conference
grammar.explanation: Apply capitals, spelling, punctuation and paragraphing.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read, mark, revise, then read again.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

writing.title: Portfolio captions
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a precise paragraph about each of three selected pieces and explain what each shows.
writing.modelText: This revised report shows that I can organise facts and support an explanation with evidence.
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence or examples; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.title: Project proposal
writing.promptAndInstructions: Explain your topic, audience, product and plan in two organised paragraphs.
writing.modelText: My project will teach younger learners about caring for nature. I will create an illustrated guide using facts and examples.

writing.support: Use a planning frame, word bank and one model from an earlier unit.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.title: Presentation notes

writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a purposeful conclusion.

writing.title: Grade 4 reflection

quizzes.question: What does 'mail' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who belongs to a country and has rights there. | Letters and packages that are sent or delivered. | To have the same opinion, or to say yes to something. | To keep doing something without stopping.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Letters and packages that are sent or delivered.
quizzes.explanation: I received a letter in the mail today.

quizzes.question: What does 'language' mean?
quizzes.options: A way people talk and write to share ideas and feelings. | To get something you want, or to become better at something. | To have the same opinion, or to say yes to something. | To keep doing something without stopping.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A way people talk and write to share ideas and feelings.
quizzes.explanation: English is a language that many people speak around the world.

quizzes.question: What does 'breath' mean?
quizzes.options: A small amount of water in the air or on a surface. | The air you take in and let out of your lungs. | The soft hair that covers some animals' bodies. | The top or outside part of something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The air you take in and let out of your lungs.
quizzes.explanation: I took a deep breath before I started to speak.

quizzes.question: What does 'sweat' mean?
quizzes.options: A deep valley with steep sides, often with a river at the bottom. | The clear water that comes from your skin when you are hot. | The soft hair that covers some animals' bodies. | The top or outside part of something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The clear water that comes from your skin when you are hot.
quizzes.explanation: After running in the sun, I wiped the sweat from my face.

A way people talk and write to share ideas and feelings. English is a language that many people speak around the world.

Food made of two pieces of bread with meat, cheese, or vegetables inside. I ate a chicken sandwich for lunch.
A young sheep, or the meat from it. We cooked lamb and rice for the guests.
Help or work done for others. The library started a free book service for the town.
To bring something to a person or place. The mail carrier will deliver our letters.
To run fast on four legs, like a horse. The horse began to gallop across the field.
To go faster, or to make something go faster. The car can accelerate when the driver presses the pedal.
